title: INmune Bio Receives FDA End-of-Phase 2 Meeting Minutes Confirming Integrated Registration Pathway for XPro1595 in Early Alzheimer's Disease
content:
INmune Bio, Inc. (NASDAQ: INMB) today announced that it received the official minutes from its End-of-Phase 2 (Type B) meeting with the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA). The minutes confirm regulatory alignment on the Company's proposed integrated Phase 2b/3 clinical development strategy for XPro1595 in early Alzheimer's Disease (AD).

Integrated Phase 2b/3 Framework: The FDA indicated no objection to the Company's proposed integrated Phase 2b/3 design under a single master protocol. The Phase 2b portion will enroll approximately 300 participants over a nine-month evaluation period designed to validate key efficacy and biomarker assumptions before expansion into the Phase 3 registration segment. The full program is expected to enroll approximately 1,000 participants, with the Phase 3 portion evaluating XPro1595 over 18 months.

CDR-SB as Sole Primary Endpoint: The FDA raised no objection to the use of Clinical Dementia Rating Scale-Sum of Boxes (CDR-SB) as the sole primary efficacy endpoint for the Phase 3 portion of the study. CDR-SB is the primary endpoint used in recently approved Alzheimer's disease therapies including lecanemab and donanemab.

Biomarker-Driven Enrichment Strategy Supported: The FDA raised no objection to the Company's inflammation-based enrichment strategy, which will enroll patients defined by two or more biomarkers associated with peripheral inflammation and immune-mediated disease risk (hsCRP, ESR, HbA1c, APOE4), which are mechanistically linked to soluble TNF signaling. This approach is designed to align patient biology with XPro1595's selective soluble TNF mechanism of action and supports a precision medicine development strategy.

Exploratory Cohort: At the FDA's recommendation, the trial will include an exploratory cohort (approximately 20% of enrollment) of non-enriched early Alzheimer's Disease patients to assess the broader effect of XPro1595. This cohort is not required to be independently powered.

About XPro1595: XPro1595 (pegipanermin) is a next-generation inhibitor of tumor necrosis factor (TNF) that neutralizes soluble TNF (sTNF), without affecting trans-membrane TNF (tmTNF) or TNF receptors. This selectivity is mechanistically critical for CNS applications: conventional pan-TNF inhibitors have historically been contraindicated in neurological indications due to tmTNF's role in neuroprotection, oligodendrocyte survival, and synaptic plasticity. The Company noted zero cases of ARIA in its Phase 2 MINDFuL study, differentiating XPro1595's safety profile from amyloid-targeting therapies. The Phase 2b assessment will be informed by the Early Mild Alzheimer's Cognitive Composite (EMACC) and plasma p-tau-217.

title: Adalimumab versus Conventional Immunosuppression for Uveitis (ADVISE) Trial
organization: The ADVISE Trial Research Group / Ophthalmology (American Academy of Ophthalmology)
content:
Purpose: Patients with noninfectious intermediate uveitis, posterior uveitis, or panuveitis typically are treated with oral corticosteroids and immunosuppressive agents such as antimetabolites and calcineurin inhibitors. The anti-TNF monoclonal antibody adalimumab is also used to treat these diseases, but its comparative effectiveness versus conventional agents was unknown. This trial compared the effectiveness of adalimumab versus conventional immunosuppressive drugs for uveitis.

Design: Randomized, unmasked, comparative effectiveness trial at uveitis clinics at academic medical centers and private practices in the United States and internationally.

Participants: 227 patients with active or recently active noninfectious intermediate uveitis, posterior uveitis, or panuveitis needing immunosuppression were randomized to adalimumab (n=114) or conventional immunosuppressive drugs — antimetabolites, calcineurin inhibitors, or both (n=113).

Main Outcome: Primary outcome was successful corticosteroid sparing, defined as inactive uveitis at a prednisone dosage of ≤7.5 mg/day for 2 consecutive study visits ≥28 days apart at 6 months.

Results: By 6 months, successful corticosteroid sparing occurred in 69% of adalimumab participants versus 54% of conventional immunosuppression participants (OR 1.86; 95% CI 1.06–3.25; P = 0.029). By 12 months, successful corticosteroid discontinuation occurred in 55% of adalimumab participants versus 40% of conventional immunosuppression participants (OR 1.85; 95% CI 1.06–3.19; P = 0.028). By 12 months, corticosteroid sparing occurred in 86% vs. 77% (OR 1.89; 95% CI 0.93–3.83; P = 0.077).

Conclusions: Adalimumab resulted in a greater proportion of participants with successful corticosteroid sparing at 6 months and successful corticosteroid discontinuation at 12 months compared with conventional immunosuppression for noninfectious uveitis. Median patient age was 44 years; 67% were women. These findings support earlier adalimumab deployment for non-infectious uveitis and strengthen the case for anti-TNF biologics over conventional immunosuppressants in this indication. Published in Ophthalmology 2026;133:310–325.

title: Do high rheumatoid factor levels impact response to certolizumab pegol in patients with inadequately controlled rheumatoid arthritis? A post hoc analysis of the phase IIIb REALISTIC trial
content:
Objectives: To assess the impact of rheumatoid factor (RF) levels and previous inadequate responses or intolerance to TNF inhibitors (TNFi-IR) on the efficacy of certolizumab pegol (CZP) in patients with RA through a post hoc analysis of the REALISTIC trial.

Background: Unlike most TNFis used for RA, certolizumab pegol does not have a fragment crystallisable (Fc) region that can be bound by RF. Among patients with higher RF levels, CZP-treated patients demonstrate higher drug concentrations compared with those with lower RF levels. Patients with RA and high RF levels have poorer prognosis, more aggressive disease, higher cardiovascular risk, and higher risk of radiographic progression.

Methods: In the phase IIIb REALISTIC trial, patients with RA were randomised to CZP (400 mg at weeks 0, 2 and 4, then 200 mg every 2 weeks) or placebo for 12 weeks, followed by open-label CZP for a minimum of 16 weeks. Outcomes were stratified by baseline RF level and previous TNFi use.

Results: 930 patients were included: 751 CZP-randomised and 179 placebo-randomised. At week 12, CZP-randomised patients experienced marked and similar improvements in disease activity irrespective of RF level and prior TNFi use, while placebo-randomised patients did not. Responses generally improved through week 36 in CZP-treated patients — including placebo-randomised switchers — with similar efficacy across subgroups. Patients with high and low RF levels experienced similar clinical responses to CZP treatment irrespective of previous inadequate responses or intolerance to TNFis.

Conclusions: These findings support CZP as an effective treatment for patients with RA who have high RF levels and prior inadequate responses to TNFis. CZP's Fc-free structure may contribute to its RF-independent activity by preventing immune complex formation between the drug and circulating RF — a mechanism that does not affect full IgG antibodies such as adalimumab and infliximab. Trial registration: NCT00717236.

title: Effect of fluoxetine on organ dysfunction and mortality in severe sepsis
organization: Ain Shams University, Cairo / PLoS ONE
content:
Background: Sepsis is a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in intensive care units, characterized by a dysregulated host response to infection. Recent evidence suggests fluoxetine, a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor, may exert immunometabolic effects beneficial in sepsis through inhibition of NF-κB activation in macrophages, reducing downstream TNF-α and IL-1β transcription.

Methods: Single-center, randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial in adult ICU patients with severe sepsis. Patients randomized to fluoxetine 40mg/day (n=23) or placebo (n=23) for up to 14 days in addition to standard care. Primary outcome was vasopressor duration; secondary outcomes included ICU length of stay, SOFA score, APACHE II score, 28-day mortality, and inflammatory markers (TNF-α, IL-1, CRP, procalcitonin) assessed at days 1, 3, 7, 10, and 14.

Results: Fluoxetine significantly reduced vasopressor duration (6.2 ± 0.4 vs. 7.9 ± 0.8 days; p < 0.001), ICU stay (15.9 ± 1.6 vs. 17.1 ± 1.1 days; p = 0.005), and inflammatory markers by day 7, including TNF-α, IL-1, CRP, and procalcitonin (all p < 0.05). SOFA and APACHE II scores were also lower in the fluoxetine group on days 7 and 10. No significant difference in 28-day mortality was observed (8.7% vs. 17.4%; p = 0.381). No statistically significant differences in baseline characteristics were observed between groups (gender p=0.767, age p=0.114).

Conclusions: Fluoxetine as adjunctive therapy in severe sepsis may reduce vasopressor dependence, attenuate inflammation, and shorten ICU stay without increasing adverse effects. Its mortality benefit remains uncertain — the trial was underpowered at n=46 for a mortality endpoint — and warrants further investigation in larger powered trials. The immunometabolic mechanism is consistent with SSRI-mediated NF-κB inhibition in immune cells.

title: The clinical outcome of Montelukast versus co-enzyme Q10 in adult patients with sepsis: A randomized controlled clinical trial
organization: Egyptian academic institutions / Journal of Critical Care Research
content:
Background: Oxidative stress and inflammatory cytokines are involved in sepsis pathogenesis. This study evaluated the potential benefits of montelukast and co-enzyme Q10 (CoQ10) in septic patients as anti-inflammatory and antioxidant adjunctive therapies.

Methods: Open-label randomized controlled trial at an ICU. Ninety adult septic patients randomized to: standard care alone (n=30, control), standard care plus montelukast sodium 10 mg/day (n=30), or standard care plus CoQ10 210 mg/day (n=30). Primary outcome: 28-day mortality. Secondary outcomes: duration of mechanical ventilation and vasopressor, SOFA score, TNF-α, and malondialdehyde (MDA). Trial registered at ClinicalTrials.gov (NCT05293132).

Results: 28-day mortality rate was 23.3% (montelukast), 33.3% (CoQ10), and 56.7% (control), with only the montelukast vs. control difference reaching statistical significance (p = 0.024). Duration of vasopressor was significantly lower in both active groups versus control: montelukast 2.9 ± 1.3 days, CoQ10 5.4 ± 2.7 days, control 9.8 ± 4.8 days (both p < 0.001). Both montelukast and CoQ10 showed significant positive effect on SOFA score, procalcitonin, CRP, TNF-α, and MDA. Montelukast showed no adverse effects. 20% of CoQ10 patients experienced hypotension.

Conclusion: Montelukast and CoQ10 were tolerable and showed potential benefits as adjunct therapies in sepsis. The 33-percentage-point mortality reduction with montelukast (p=0.024) is compelling but requires interpretation with caution given the open-label design and single-center setting. The mechanism involves montelukast's CysLT1 receptor antagonism, which blocks leukotriene-mediated downstream TNF-α signaling and inflammatory amplification cascades in sepsis.

title: Maraviroc treatment for hospitalized participants with non-severe COVID-19 at risk of progression: a randomized, proof-of-concept clinical trial
organization: Virgen del Rocío University Hospital, Seville, Spain / Journal of Microbiology, Immunology and Infection
content:
Background: Therapeutic options for hospitalized people with COVID-19 remain limited, especially for non-severe patients at risk of progression. The anti-inflammatory role of maraviroc, a CCR5 antagonist, makes it a candidate for COVID-19 therapeutic strategies by reducing immune cell trafficking to sites of inflammation and suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine cascades including TNF-α and IL-6.

Methods: Proof-of-concept phase II, parallel, open-label clinical trial evaluating maraviroc 300 mg twice daily combined with standard of care (SoC) versus SoC alone for 14 days in hospitalized patients with mild COVID-19 with pneumonia and oxygen saturation ≥94% on ambient air. 44 participants recruited; 11 excluded; 33 included (control n=17, maraviroc n=16).

Results: The proportion of participants experiencing COVID-19 progression was 2.8 times higher in the control group than in the maraviroc group. Three control participants were admitted to intensive care unit versus none in the maraviroc group. The only variable associated with time to severe COVID-19 progression was maraviroc treatment. The median time on oxygen therapy was 11 days in the control group; the two maraviroc patients who required oxygen had oxygen therapy for 1 and 4 days. Grade 3–4 events were only present in the control group (respiratory failure n=2, hypertransaminasemia n=2). Maraviroc treatment was associated with better neutrophil/lymphocyte ratio and lower lactate dehydrogenase, IL-6, and TNF-α levels at day 7.

Conclusions: A beneficial role of maraviroc was observed in hospitalized participants with mild COVID-19 at risk of progression. Small sample size requires confirmation in a larger randomized trial. Maraviroc's established HIV safety profile provides a foundation for repurposing as a cytokine storm modulator in infectious disease settings. Published in Journal of Microbiology, Immunology and Infection 59 (2026) 54–61.

title: Safety and pharmacokinetics of SOR102, an oral bispecific inhibitor of TNF and interleukin-23 in healthy participants and patients with ulcerative colitis: a first-in-human, double-blind, randomised, placebo-controlled, phase 1 trial
content:
Background: SOR102 is an oral bispecific antibody comprised of single-domain antibodies targeting TNF and interleukin (IL)-23p19. This phase 1 first-in-human trial assessed the safety, pharmacokinetics, and preliminary clinical activity of SOR102 in healthy participants and patients with active ulcerative colitis.

Methods: Three-part trial in the UK, Ukraine, and Georgia. Part 1: healthy participants randomized 6:2 to single ascending doses (135 mg, 405 mg, 1215 mg, 3645 mg SOR102) or placebo. Part 2: healthy participants randomized 8:2 to SOR102 1215 mg twice daily or placebo for 7 days. Part 3: UC patients randomized 1:1:1 to SOR102 810 mg once daily, SOR102 810 mg twice daily, or placebo for 42 days. 64 total participants enrolled across all parts (SOR102 n=48, placebo n=16).

Findings: In part 1, TEAEs occurred in 3 (13%) SOR102 vs. 2 (25%) placebo participants; only mild diarrhoea was treatment-related. No TEAEs in part 2. In part 3 (UC patients) at day 42: Mayo score clinical response achieved in 3/7 (43%) once-daily, 5/9 (56%) twice-daily, and 1/6 (17%) placebo patients. Symptomatic remission in 43% (once-daily), 56% (twice-daily), 0% (placebo). No clinically significant effects on ECG, vital signs, or laboratory parameters; no deaths.

Interpretation: Favourable safety profile and preliminary efficacy support continued development of SOR102 for UC. SOR102 is the first oral bispecific targeting TNF-α and IL-23p19 to reach human trials — all currently approved biologics for UC are injectable or infused. An oral formulation with dual pathway blockade addresses both the injection burden and the mechanistic limitation of single-target therapy. Phase 2 planning is underway. Published in Lancet Gastroenterology & Hepatology 2026;11:34–45 (PMID 41242336).

title: Anti-TNF Therapies Promote a Proximal-to-Distal Healing Pattern in Moderate-to-Severe Ulcerative Colitis
organization: McMaster University / Inflammatory Bowel Diseases (Oxford Academic / Crohn's & Colitis Foundation)
content:
Background: Ulcerative colitis typically extends continuously from the rectum proximally. The segmental healing pattern induced by anti-TNF therapy and the optimal endoscopic scoring methodology for detecting treatment effects in UC trials are not well established.

Methods: Post hoc analysis of HIBISCUS I/II and GARDENIA trials in UC patients with moderate-to-severe disease (MES >2 up to the descending colon). 300 participants included; 217 received infliximab or adalimumab, 83 received placebo. Endoscopic improvement assessed using conventional Mayo Endoscopic Score (MES) and a 3-segment MES approach (ascending colon, descending colon, rectum). Combined endpoint: MES ≤1 plus PRO2 normalization versus PRO2 alone.

Results: Healing followed a proximal-to-distal pattern, with the highest endoscopic improvement in the descending colon and the lowest in the rectum. Infliximab-treated patients continued this trend at week 54. Anti-TNF therapy significantly improved endoscopic improvement vs. placebo (42.9% vs. 19.3%; P < 0.001). No segmental MES approach outperformed conventional MES for detecting treatment effects. Combined endpoints (MES ≤1 + PRO2 normalization) better captured therapeutic benefits than PRO2 alone (28.6% vs. 13.3%; P = 0.006).

Conclusions: UC healing follows a proximal-to-distal pattern under anti-TNF therapy. Conventional MES remains superior for detecting treatment effects over segmental MES. The finding that combined MES + PRO2 endpoints capture significantly more treatment responders than PRO2 alone has direct implications for endpoint selection in future UC anti-TNF registration trials. Published in Inflammatory Bowel Diseases 2026;32:87–96.

title: Effect of Dexmedetomidine on Endothelial Glycocalyx in Patients Undergoing Cardiac Surgery with Cardiopulmonary Bypass
organization: Journal of Cardiothoracic and Vascular Anesthesia
content:
Objective: To evaluate the protective role of dexmedetomidine on endothelial glycocalyx in cardiac surgery patients undergoing cardiopulmonary bypass (CPB).

Design: Randomized controlled trial at a single center (June–December 2022).

Participants: 58 patients undergoing elective valvular cardiac surgery with CPB. Randomized to dexmedetomidine (n=29) or saline infusion (n=29), starting 10 minutes before anesthesia induction and continuing throughout the maintenance phase.

Endpoints: Primary: plasma syndecan-1 concentration (marker of glycocalyx shedding). Secondary: heparan sulfate, TNF-α levels, and long-term clinical outcomes. Samples collected at T1 (before induction), T2 (5 min after aortic unclamping), T3 (1 hour postoperatively), T4 (24 hours postoperatively).

Results: The dexmedetomidine group showed significantly less glycocalyx shedding versus controls, an effect that persisted throughout CPB and recovery. TNF-α levels were significantly lower at T2–T3 in the dexmedetomidine group, along with reduced lactate levels at aortic unclamping.

Conclusions: Dexmedetomidine preserves endothelial glycocalyx integrity and attenuates systemic inflammation in CPB patients. The glycocalyx-protective effect — evidenced by reduced syndecan-1 shedding — represents a mechanistically novel anti-inflammatory finding beyond conventional cytokine suppression. The mechanism involves α2-adrenergic receptor-mediated attenuation of the sympathoadrenal stress response during ischemia-reperfusion, which reduces endothelial activation and downstream TNF-α release during the high-stress period of aortic unclamping.

title: Effects of esketamine combined with dexmedetomidine on oxidative stress and lung function during one-lung ventilation in patients with mild to moderate COPD undergoing thoracoscopic surgery
organization: Second Affiliated Hospital of Fujian Medical University / International Immunopharmacology
content:
Background: This study evaluated lung-protective effects of esketamine and dexmedetomidine administered before anesthesia induction in mild-to-moderate COPD patients undergoing video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS).

Methods: 60 COPD patients randomly assigned to four groups (n=15 each): Control (Group C, saline), Dexmedetomidine (Group D, 0.8 μg/kg), Esketamine (Group E, 0.25 mg/kg), Combination (Group ED, 0.8 μg/kg dexmedetomidine + 0.25 mg/kg esketamine). Inflammatory markers (IL-6, IL-8, IL-10, TNF-α), oxidative stress markers (MDA, SOD), and lung mechanics (Ppeak, Pplat, Cdyn) were assessed.

Results at T4 (2 hours after one-lung ventilation): Peak airway pressure (Ppeak) and plateau pressure (Pplat) were significantly lower, and dynamic lung compliance (Cdyn) significantly higher, in Groups E and ED versus C and D (P < 0.05). TNF-α levels were significantly lower in Groups E and ED versus C, and significantly lower in Group ED versus D (P < 0.05). IL-6 and IL-8 were significantly lower in Groups E and ED versus C and D. IL-10 was significantly higher in Groups E and ED. MDA significantly reduced and SOD elevated in Groups E and ED. PaO2 at T4 was significantly higher in Group ED versus C and D (P < 0.05).

Conclusions: The esketamine + dexmedetomidine combination improved intraoperative pulmonary mechanics and oxygenation and attenuated TNF-α-driven inflammation and oxidative stress in COPD patients undergoing VATS. The additive anti-inflammatory effect reflects two mechanistically distinct pathways: esketamine's NMDA receptor antagonism reduces neuroinflammatory cytokine release including TNF-α, while dexmedetomidine's α2-agonism reduces sympathoadrenal-driven cytokine storm during one-lung ventilation. Published in International Immunopharmacology 168 (2026) 115860.

title: Impact of Alpha Lipoic Acid as an Adjuvant Therapy on Inflammation and Fibrosis in Type 2 Diabetic Patients with Ischemic Cardiomyopathy: A Randomized Controlled Trial
organization: Ain Shams University / Journal of Dietary Supplements
content:
Background: Ischemic cardiomyopathy (ICM) involves oxidative stress, inflammation, and apoptosis. Alpha-lipoic acid (ALA) has antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects, but its impact on left ventricular dysfunction and myocardial fibrosis was unclear.

Methods: Double-blind, randomized, placebo-controlled trial. 67 diabetic patients with ICM enrolled; 60 completed the study (ALA group n=30; placebo group n=30, aged 45–75 years; 70% male). ALA 600 mg once daily or placebo for 3 months, in addition to standard ICM medications. Outcomes: TNF-α, CRP, TGF-β1, MMP-2, and echocardiographic indices (LVEF, LVESD, LVEDD, LAD) before and after treatment.

Results: After 3 months, the ALA group showed significantly lower: TNF-α [443 (326/515) vs. 499 (448/657) pg/ml]; CRP [4.5 (4.2/6.1) vs. 11 (6.3/12.1) mg/l]; TGF-β1 [161 (104/189) vs. 206 (158/248) pg/ml]; MMP-2 [1450 (1164/1894) vs. 1815 (1339/2133) pg/ml] (all p < 0.05). The ALA group showed a significant increase in LVEF [36% (34/40) vs. 28% (25/31.8%)] and significant decreases in LVESD [4.7 vs. 5.6 cm], LVEDD [6.1 ± 0.7 vs. 6.5 ± 0.5 cm], and LAD [4.2 vs. 4.8 cm] (all p < 0.05).

Conclusion: Adjunctive ALA therapy reduced inflammation and improved cardiac function in diabetic patients with ICM. The TGF-β1 and MMP-2 reductions suggest potential anti-fibrotic cardiac effects beyond systemic inflammation reduction, consistent with NF-κB inhibition as ALA's primary anti-inflammatory mechanism: ALA scavenges reactive oxygen species and inhibits NF-κB transcriptional activation, thereby reducing downstream TNF-α expression.

title: Colchicine reduces inflammatory cytokines and improves symptoms in HFpEF: an observational pilot study
organization: Hebei Medical University / Frontiers in Medicine
content:
Objective: To investigate whether colchicine could safely and effectively reduce inflammatory marker levels and improve prognosis in patients with heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF).

Methods: HFpEF patients confirmed by hemodynamic measurement (LVEDP > 15 mmHg at rest or ≥19 mmHg on straight leg raising test when resting LVEDP was 10–15 mmHg) enrolled from First Hospital of Hebei Medical University. IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α assessed; patients with elevated markers treated with colchicine 0.5 mg once daily. 126 patients met inclusion criteria. Inflammatory markers reassessed at 2 weeks; HAMA, HAMD, KCCQ assessed at 1 month.

Results: After 2 weeks of colchicine, IL-8 decreased by −28.65 (95% CI: −54.20 to −11.47, p = 0.0010), a 65.5% reduction (95% CI: −82.0% to −45.0%). IL-6 decreased by −1.925 (95% CI: −4.510 to −0.29, p = 0.0028), a 30.5% reduction. After 1 month, quality of life (KCCQ) increased by 10 points (95% CI: 9.0 to 12.0, p < 0.0001); anxiety (HAMA) decreased by −1 (p < 0.0001); depression (HAMD) decreased by −1 (p < 0.0001). Safety: 4.1% adverse reaction rate, all mild (diarrhoea/flatulence in 4 patients; transient eGFR decrease in 3 patients, all resolved with dose adjustment).

Conclusion: Colchicine can safely and effectively reduce inflammatory markers such as IL-8 and IL-6 in HFpEF, with secondary symptomatic benefits in anxiety, depression, and quality of life. This is the first study to explore colchicine in HFpEF specifically. The mechanism involves inhibition of microtubule polymerization, blocking NLRP3 inflammasome activation and downstream IL-1β, IL-6, and TNF-α signaling. An observational design limits causal interpretation; a randomized trial is needed. Published in Frontiers in Medicine 12:1702293.

title: Cannabidiol exerts antiinflammatory effects but maintains T effector memory cell differentiation in humans
organization: Indiana University School of Medicine / JCI Insight
content:
Background: Cannabidiol (CBD) is increasingly used for pain management in transplant recipients with limited analgesic options. Its immunomodulatory effects in humans are not well defined at a single-cell level at CBD steady state with concomitant tacrolimus treatment.

Methods: Phase 1 ex vivo study. 23 participants provided blood samples before CBD and at steady state after receiving oral CBD (Epidiolex) up to 5 mg/kg twice daily for 11 days. Lymphocytes were stimulated with anti-CD3/CD28 antibodies, with or without tacrolimus (5 ng/mL). Assessments included CellTiter-Glo proliferation, single-cell RNA sequencing, cytokine assays, and flow cytometry.

Results: Post-CBD, T cell proliferation was significantly reduced (ratio 1.5 ± 0.571 vs. pre-CBD 2.04 ± 0.574; P = 0.0085), consistent with a ~54% reduction in cell proliferation from 11 days of CBD. An increased proportion of T effector memory (TEM) cells post-CBD was observed (22% increase), correlating with CBD plasma concentrations (R = 0.77, P = 0.01). CBD at steady state reduced IL-2 and TNF-α expression in T effector memory cells and reduced lymphotoxin alpha (LTA), while elevating anti-inflammatory IL-10. Additive immunosuppressive effects with tacrolimus were observed: combined CBD + tacrolimus produced greater suppression of T-cell activation than either agent alone.

Conclusions: CBD exerts anti-inflammatory effects in human transplant recipients, with a mixed immunomodulatory profile. The reduction in TNF-α, IL-2, and LTA alongside IL-10 elevation is pro-immunosuppressive, while maintained TEM cell differentiation suggests CBD does not impair long-term immune memory. The additive immunosuppression with tacrolimus is clinically significant and should be monitored to avoid excessive immunosuppression in calcineurin inhibitor-dependent transplant patients. Published in JCI Insight 2026;11(1):e198590.

title: Effect of liraglutide on depressive symptoms in overweight or obese patients with type 2 diabetes: protocol for a pilot randomized controlled trial
organization: Chengdu Shuangliu Hospital of Traditional Chinese Medicine / Frontiers in Endocrinology
content:
Introduction: Patients with concurrent obesity, type 2 diabetes, and depression experience high disease severity and prevalence. This triad compromises quality of life and treatment adherence. Liraglutide, a GLP-1 receptor agonist, offers benefits beyond glucose-lowering and weight reduction, with emerging evidence suggesting antidepressant effects. The risk of depression in T2DM patients is 2–3 times higher than the general population; 68.2% of T2DM patients exhibit depressive symptoms in some studies.

Protocol: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled pilot trial. 60 eligible participants randomly assigned (1:1) to liraglutide (initiated at 0.6 mg/day, titrated weekly to a maximum of 1.8 mg/day) or matched placebo, as an adjunct to standard care for 12 weeks. Primary endpoints: blood glucose, HbA1c, BMI, Hamilton Depression Rating Scale score, and resting-state fMRI metrics. Secondary endpoints: TNF-α, IL-6, superoxide dismutase, malondialdehyde, HOMA-IR, insulin sensitivity index, and HOMA-β.

Rationale for TNF-α as Secondary Outcome: GLP-1 receptor agonists including liraglutide reduce insulin resistance and adipose tissue-mediated TNF-α and inflammatory cytokine expression. Neuroinflammatory TNF-α signaling has been implicated in depression pathophysiology. The inclusion of TNF-α and IL-6 as secondary inflammatory biomarkers reflects the hypothesis that liraglutide's potential antidepressant effects may be partially mediated through neuro-inflammatory pathway modulation.

Status: Protocol published January 21, 2026. No efficacy or safety results available. This is a pilot study; primary intent is effect size estimation for future adequately powered trials. Published in Frontiers in Endocrinology 16:1629157.

title: Azathioprine combined with either rifaximin or infliximab for inflammatory bowel disease: Differential impacts on intestinal barrier function, inflammatory response and stress injury
content:
Background: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) is an immune-related chronic intestinal inflammatory disease with increasing incidence. This prospective cohort study compared rifaximin plus azathioprine (RIF+AZA) versus infliximab plus azathioprine (IFX+AZA) in patients with active IBD.

Methods: 130 patients randomized into two groups treated for 12 weeks. Outcomes assessed: intestinal barrier function [diamine oxidase (DAO), fecal calprotectin (FC), lipopolysaccharide (LPS)], mucosal repair markers [EGF, TGF-β1], inflammatory factors [IL-6, TNF-α, CRP], and oxidative stress indices [SOD, MDA].

Results: IFX+AZA rapidly reduced pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α decreased, CRP normalized) and improved mucosal injury markers (DAO improved), but elevated LPS levels (P<0.05) — suggesting acute anti-inflammatory efficacy at the cost of some intestinal barrier disruption. RIF+AZA enhanced mucosal repair (EGF and TGF-β1 improved) and antioxidant capacity (SOD elevated, MDA reduced), with less liver enzyme elevation. IFX+AZA was superior for acute TNF-α-driven inflammation control during induction; RIF+AZA offered superior mucosal healing and oxidative balance for maintenance.

Conclusion: IFX+AZA for rapid induction remission and RIF+AZA for maintenance therapy supports a complementary two-phase sequential strategy for IBD, rather than viewing these regimens as competing alternatives. The differential impact on intestinal barrier function — anti-TNF induction temporarily elevating LPS despite cytokine suppression — has practical implications for transition timing between induction and maintenance. Published in Pakistan Journal of Pharmaceutical Sciences 2026;39(3). PMID: 41620899.

title: Navigator Medicines Announces FDA IND Clearance for NAV-240 Phase 2a Trial in Hidradenitis Suppurativa and Australian HREC Approval for NAV-242 Phase 1 First-in-Human Study
content:
Navigator Medicines today announced two significant clinical program milestones for its OX40L/TNF-α bispecific antibody franchise.

NAV-240 Phase 2a IND Clearance: The U.S. Food and Drug Administration has cleared the IND application for NAV-240 to enter a Phase 2a clinical trial in patients with moderate-to-severe hidradenitis suppurativa (HS). Dosing in the Phase 2a trial has been initiated in Q1 2026. NAV-240 is a bispecific antibody that simultaneously blocks OX40L and TNF-α — two inflammatory mediators that together drive the chronic relapsing inflammatory cascades characteristic of HS. The dual blockade mechanism is designed to deliver greater disease control than TNF inhibition alone. NAV-240 builds on a positive Phase 1 safety and tolerability profile; Phase 1b multiple ascending dose (MAD) results are expected to be presented at an upcoming medical congress. HS is a chronic, debilitating inflammatory skin condition characterized by recurrent painful nodules and abscesses, affecting approximately 1% of the global population, with significant unmet need given the limited durability of current anti-TNF monotherapy.

NAV-242 Australian HREC Approval: The Australian Human Research Ethics Committee (HREC) has approved the Phase 1 first-in-human program for NAV-242, a next-generation, half-life-extended OX40L/TNF-α bispecific antibody. NAV-242 is engineered with extended half-life to optimize plasma exposure and reduce dosing frequency compared to NAV-240. Dosing in the Phase 1a single ascending dose (SAD) study in healthy volunteers and HS patients was initiated Q1 2026. SAD results expected Q4 2026. NAV-242's indication scope includes HS, Crohn's disease, and ulcerative colitis, reflecting Navigator's strategy to position the next-generation molecule as a platform asset across multiple immune-mediated inflammatory diseases. Both NAV-240 and NAV-242 represent the first OX40L/TNF-α dual-blocking bispecific antibodies to reach late-stage clinical development.

title: Nurix Therapeutics Provides Corporate Pipeline Update: GS-6791 (IRAK4 Degrader, Gilead) Phase 1 Ongoing; NX-3911 (STAT6 Degrader, Sanofi) IND Filing Anticipated 2026
content:
Nurix Therapeutics (NASDAQ: NRIX) provided a corporate pipeline update highlighting advancement of two partnered degrader programs relevant to inflammatory and immunological disease biology.

GS-6791 (IRAK4 Degrader, Gilead Partnership): GS-6791 is an oral, selective IRAK4 (Interleukin-1 Receptor-Associated Kinase 4) protein degrader co-developed by Nurix and Gilead Sciences. IRAK4 is a serine/threonine kinase that functions as an obligate signaling node downstream of Toll-like receptors (TLRs) and the IL-1 receptor family. IRAK4 activation drives NF-κB nuclear translocation and subsequent transcription of pro-inflammatory mediators including TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-12. By degrading IRAK4 protein — rather than transiently inhibiting its kinase activity — GS-6791 aims to achieve more complete and sustained suppression of NF-κB/TNF-α signaling. GS-6791 is currently in Phase 1 dose escalation in healthy volunteers, with results expected in 2026. Nurix retains 50/50 U.S. co-development and co-commercialization options.

NX-3911 (STAT6 Degrader, Sanofi Partnership): NX-3911 is an oral, selective STAT6 protein degrader co-developed by Nurix and Sanofi targeting the IL-4/IL-13 signaling axis. STAT6 mediates signaling downstream of IL-4 and IL-13 — key cytokines in type 2 inflammation that interact with and amplify TNF-alpha-driven inflammatory cascades in atopic and allergic conditions. An IND filing by Sanofi is anticipated in 2026. Nurix retains 50/50 U.S. co-development options. Both programs use Nurix's proprietary DELigase platform for targeted protein degradation. If GS-6791 Phase 1 data demonstrate tolerable safety and target engagement, it would represent the first clinical proof-of-concept for upstream IRAK4 degradation as an alternative to direct TNF-α neutralization in I&I indications.
